data.title: Nvidia Remains A Generational Winner
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) remains the queen of AI infrastructure, where it commands the AI board with unmatched compute, networking, and system prowess. In my initial coverage of Nvidia, I crowned Nvidia as the queen on the AI chessboard. My reasoning was that it's the piece that moves in all directions, dominating every corner of AI compute. And for sure, after Q2 FY 2026 earnings, that metaphor holds even truer. The company went ahead to deliver record 2Q revenue worth $46.7Bn which is a 56% increase y/y.

This actually validates the explosive AI demand driving its growth. Specifically, data center sales surged to $41.1Bn. Also, I note that Nvidia's Blackwell platform has started translating into real numbers and is also a leap in investor confidence. And in this regard, I am maintaining my Buy Rating.

Nvidia's management has radiated some confidence. And despite the sky-high expectations, NVDA beat its own guidance and even projected more than 42% revenue growth over the next year. As the most valuable company on earth, this obviously surpassed the ~10% norm for Nasdaq tech companies, as per Bloomberg Intelligence. I believe Nvidia's leadership in AI infrastructure is only the beginning, and Q2's results underscore that the company is on a trajectory of sustained and unprecedented growth.

As of writing this, Nvidia's market cap stands at $4.14Tn. And still at the top as the most valuable company on earth. So, what makes the company warrant a Buy rating even while still at the top?

Well, there are a couple of catalysts and opportunities that I believe will continue pulling the company to a higher market cap.

The Catalysts

Despite the already high growth, I believe Nvidia's true opportunity is still in its early innings. And we are standing at the cusp of what Jensen Huang might call "the big bang of intelligence":

"...We are at the big bang of intelligence, and the United Kingdom’s Goldilocks ecosystem of world-class expertise, outstanding universities and vibrant industries is uniquely positioned to thrive in the age of AI. With AI supercomputers powering state-of-the-art models locally, a new generation of U.K. researchers, developers and entrepreneurs will drive discovery and build the companies of tomorrow..."

Jensen Huang, CEO, Nvidia & UK's Announced Collaboration.

This clearly implies that the demand for AI infrastructure is projected to be astronomical. In fact, during the Q2 call, the CEO sized it in no uncertain terms by saying:

"...Over the next 5 years, we're going to scale into with Blackwell, with Rubin and follow-ons to scale into effectively a $3 trillion to $4 trillion AI infrastructure opportunity...."

Jensen Huang, CEO, 2Q FY 2026 Earnings Call.

And this means that by 2030, annual AI spending could rival and exceed traditional IT spending. Just to provide context, a report by Deloitte estimates $5.2Tn of AI data center capex will be required by 2030 in order to meet compute demand. And this is a multi-trillion-dollar tide lifting NVDA's boat. And with roughly 70% market share in AI compute, Nvidia is well positioned to seize a disproportionate chunk of that spending.

So the question is, what fuels this massive total addressable market, or TAM?

Well, a proliferation of AI use cases and an explosion in model complexity and usage. For instance, in 2023 and 2024, much of the focus was on LLMs. But now, the narrative is shifting to inference (which is actually deploying AI models into everyday services). In very simple terms, inferencing is where real volume will take place. And this is because serving millions of people requires orders of magnitude more compute than the number that was used to train them. Already, NVDA has noted that AI token generation has now increased almost 10x in the last year alone. Companies such as Microsoft (MSFT) went from processing 10Tn tokens a month to 100Tn in its Azure cloud within a year. And remember, each token processed is basically work that is done by GPUs.

And what's even more interesting is agentic AI, which doesn't just respond to one prompt only but can plan, take actions, and perform multi-step tasks. And this is why I believe that as AI evolves to be more interactive and autonomous, then the demand for inference compute will also be increasing beyond the current projected growth rates. And for sure, this is one of the most underappreciated drivers for Nvidia. The way I see it is that we are not just going to have AI users, but rather will have AI doing more tasks per user and consuming far more GPU cycles. Again, I want you to remember that every time an AI agent plans a complex task, it translates to thousands of GPU operations where previously there might have been only one.

"...demand only grows. One shot chatbots have evolved into reasoning agentic AI that research, plan and use tools, driving orders of magnitude jump in compute for both training and inference. Agentic AI is reaching maturity and has opened the enterprise market to build domain and company-specific AI agents for enterprise workflows, products and services..."

Jensen Huang, CEO, 2Q FY 2026 Earnings Call.

So now that the narrative is shifting to inferencing, Nvidia is positioning itself to capture this wave. For instance, it recently announced Rubin CPX. Which is a new class of inference-focused GPUs, alongside its next-gen Rubin architecture. And when I look at the commercials, I note that Nvidia's $100Mn of investment in a Rubin AI system could yield $5Bn in annual revenue for an AI service provider. And this is why I believe this 50X ratio shows how valuable AI compute is.

"...We expect annual AI infrastructure investments to continue growing, driven by the several factors: reasoning agentic AI requiring orders of magnitude more training and inference compute, global build- outs for sovereign AI, enterprise AI adoption, and the arrival of physical AI and robotics..."

Colette Kress, CFO, 2Q FY 2026 Earnings Call.

Beyond cloud and enterprise, there are definitely new verticals emerging for AI often. I will not dwell on all of them, but just to mention some of them, such as physical AI. Another instance is sovereign AI, which is nations and governmental organizations building their AI supercomputers for national use. As an example, just recently UK's collaboration with Nvidia to build an AI infrastructure featuring more than 120K Blackwell GPUs.

In summary, I would say that the catalyst is the AI revolution itself. And it's fast accelerating. And Nvidia is literally at the heart of the revolution. It's not just enjoying a one-time boom. But instead, it's creating new markets with its innovations.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, I'd argue that the valuation remains reasonable given the growth and margin. I note that NVDA scores an F valuation grade.

However, what catches my attention is the PEG, which validates my thesis. The company has an FWD non-GAAP PEG of 1.06x, while the sector median stands at 1.86x. This suggests that Nvidia is 43% discounted relative to the sector median. And this clearly convinces me that the company's valuation is not outlandish. The company is expected to grow by 45% to 60% annually in the next few years, while paying ~40x earnings for that kind of growth is in line with growth companies historically.

I would say that Nvidia's valuation reflects excellence. And by traditional measures, it is quite expensive. However, those measures don't fully account for the unprecedented growth and profitability combination that Nvidia has.

Nvidia's Key Risk

At a $4.14Tn market cap, there is definitely a valuation risk. As of now, Nvidia's stock is priced for high growth, and this means that any hiccup, whether it's a growth slowdown or broader market rotation, could cause volatility. At FWD non-GAAP P/E of 37x, it means expectations are quite high. Nevertheless, if NVDA continues with a brilliant execution, then definitely, the earnings will justify the price.

Final Thoughts

Usually in chess, when you have a dominant position and control the board, the wise move is often to press the advantage. And that's exactly what Nvidia is doing. It's pressing its advantage in AI infrastructure. And despite being the most valuable company at $4.14Tn market cap, there is still so much more that NVDA has to unpack and lead the AI revolution from the front.

data.title: Breaking Down The Bearish Narrative
data.description:
The bears have been beaten down the past three years, but they have not yet hibernated. The tariff-induced correction during the first quarter of this year fueled fears of recession and an end to the bull market, which breathed new life into their narrative. Yet the post-Liberation Day rally, which is now in its sixth month, has been one for the record books, with the S&P 500 surging more than 30% to multiple new all-time highs. I was concerned this summer that the president’s tariffs would revive inflation and slow growth to such a degree that the economy would face a period of stagflation. To some degree, that occurred. The rate of inflation has ticked up three of the past four months, and growth slowed dramatically during the first half of this year, with job creation stagnant the past four months. I feared that might open the door to a pullback at best or another correction at worst, but neither came to pass. Still, there were no indications that the economic expansion was at risk, and this is the bedrock of the bull market.

The underlying premise of the bearish narrative is that valuations for the broad market are excessive. I can’t deny that fact. The cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings [CAPE] ratio for the S&P 500 is currently at 37.5, which makes this the third most expensive market on record.

The problem is that valuations are a horrible timing tool. John Maynard Keynes told us that “the market can remain irrational longer than you can remain solvent,” assuming you are betting against the market. In other words, the market can stay expensive for a very long time, defying what we perceive to be fair value, due to a multitude of factors. Once valuations are excessive, you need to see negative rates of change in the vital signs for the economy to such an extent that the expansion is in peril. That usually requires excesses in the economy that need to be extinguished. I can’t identify any excesses today, but I think the most likely one in the future will be related to the buildout of AI. Valuations on their own are not a good reason to avoid the stock market, and minimal exposure will likely lead to very subpar returns. Where valuations do become relevant is in predicting forward returns.

The forward returns for the market show losses across all time frames when the CAPE multiple is above 35, as it is today. Obviously, the returns improve dramatically when we start from lower and lower valuations. This is an ominous statistic for buy-and-hold investors who passively invest new money in an index today. I have a lot more confidence in these numbers over a 3-10 year time period than I do the next 12 months. That is because another statistic I shared last week shows that when the Fed has cut rates with the S&P 500 within 2% of an all-time high, the index has been higher every single time one year later. Remember that these forward returns are averages.

This data is consistent with the chart below, which I first shared with investors two years ago. It suggests that we may be in the early innings of a secular bear market, as we were from 2000 through 2010. The culprits this time would probably be higher rates of inflation and rising long-term interest rates. This isn’t as bearish as it looks. Within a secular bear market, there can be multiple cyclical bull markets. During the last secular bear, there was a very profitable bull run from 2003 through 2007. That is a long time to sit out of the market if you are bearish.

The best strategy to employ if we are in a secular bear market is a tactical one. That means be selective about the securities you own, maintain cash in portfolios to be opportunistic, stay diversified across asset classes, and maintain international exposure as well. There is always a bull market somewhere, and it is a tactical portfolio's mission to find it.

data.title: AstraZeneca: Consistent Growth And Upcoming Catalysts Make This A Buy
data.description:
The Cambridge, UK-based AstraZeneca PLC (AZN) was founded in 1999 as an amalgamation of the pharmaceutical companies Zeneca Group, based in Britain, and Astra AB, from Sweden. The company’s primary listing is in the UK (LSE: AZN), but it is also listed on the Nasdaq (through ADR shares) and Stockholm exchanges. It has six main R&D centers, in Cambridge, UK, Gothenburg, Sweden, Gaithersburg, MD, Boston, MA, and two in China, Beijing and Shanghai.

AstraZeneca’s main focus, historically, has been on oncology, antiviral therapies, and inflammatory diseases, but in 2013 after a major corporate restructure, the company consolidated its focus into three key areas: 1) respiratory inflammation and autoimmunity; 2) cardiovascular and metabolic diseases; and 3) oncology. They also moved into rare diseases with their buyout of Alexion Pharmaceuticals in 2021.

Based on 2024 revenues (USD), AstraZeneca was ranked #5 globally among all pharmaceutical/biotech companies, behind Merck (MRK), Pfizer (PFE), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and Abbvie (ABBV), and #4 in terms of current market cap, which stands at $236 billion. In the most recent reporting period (1H 2025), the company generated 43% of its revenue in the US, with Europe at 21%.

In terms of treatment areas, the company derived the largest share of its revenue (43%) from its oncology division, which also enjoyed the strongest growth (21% on a TTM basis). Its 6 main revenue-generating drugs all enjoyed revenue growth year-on-year, which shows it is at the forefront of this treatment area. Tagrisso, its best-selling treatment for small-cell lung cancer, generated $3.49 billion in revenue and enjoyed 10% growth globally. Of the global list of top-selling drugs in 2024, it was ranked #22. Only AstraZeneca’s SGLT2 inhibitor (Farxiga) for the treatment of type 2 diabetes, chronic (long-term) heart failure and chronic kidney disease, ranked #20 on the 2024 list, earned more. It generated $4.21 billion in revenue in H1 2025, showing an even more impressive 13% growth.

Also worth mentioning in its oncology segment is Imfinzi, its immune checkpoint inhibitor for the treatment of lung and various other cancers ($2.72 billion in 1H 2025, at 21% growth), its PARP inhibitor Lynparza ($1.56 billion in H1 2025 revenue at 9% growth), and Calquence, approved to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia ($1.26 billion revenue in 1H 2025, also at 9% growth). Most exciting in their current offering is Enhurtu for the treatment of breast cancer, which enjoyed a whopping 38% sales growth year over year.

Enhurtu takes a novel approach to treating breast cancer, the most common and deadliest form of cancer for women. The drug targets the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2), which is present in a large number of breast cancer diagnoses and poses a significant risk if patients develop brain metastases, which happens in about a third of cases. Enhurtu showed very promising data in recent trials, with survival rates that handily beat out its chief rival Kadcyla, made by Genentech, part of Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY). In my estimation, Enhurtu is set to see sustained revenue growth going forward – GlobalData estimates $11.2 billion in annual sales by 2030 (representing a CAGR of over 20% from 2024 sales figures) – but oncology is an incredibly competitive space and rival companies are progressing treatments through trials at a breathtaking pace. Encouragingly, Enhurtu has been approved for other indications including gastric, non small-cell lung cancer and any HER2-positive solid tumors. The company is continuing trials to increase the number of indications for this blockbuster drug that, in time, could overtake even Farxiga and Tagrisso in annual sales.

In its respiratory and immunology division, Symbicort (1.44 billion 1H 2025 revenue) remained the largest contributor, but saw a 2% revenue decrease year over year. Ultomiris, for the treatment of several rare autoimmune and blood-related conditions, remains the largest revenue driver in its rare diseases portfolio, enjoying 24% revenue growth and $2.23 billion in 1H 2025. In total, AstraZeneca generated revenue of at least $170 million each in 17 different products. Their diversification significantly de-risks their portfolio, especially as drugs reach a patent cliff and generics steal market share. Their strong pipeline seems more than likely to offset lost sales due to generic competition. However, their diversification also means that the potential impact of a single product may be limited, even if it outperforms expectations. In all, the company seems to be firing on all cylinders, and this is reflected in their sustained revenue growth over the last few years. The enormous revenue growth generated from Tagrisso alongside its Covid vaccine Vaxzevria (generating $3.9 billion) in 2021 has slowed but, with its new offerings, growth has returned somewhat.

The company’s operating expenses have increased over time, but, encouragingly, most of this increase has come from R&D. As seen in the chart below, the proportion of their revenue being directed to R&D has increased, while the opposite is the case for the S,G&A expenses. This is a positive, showing increasing efficiencies in their business and a strong and sustained focus on long-term growth.

The company’s gross margin has remained consistent in the 81-83% range over recent years (currently 82.4%), while its net margin has improved – on a TTM basis – to 14.7%, its highest since 2017. Also, its EPS has ticked up nicely, showing almost 29% growth on a TTM basis.

With these metrics in hand, we would expect to see a steady appreciation in its share price, and this is certainly the case. Its 10-year chart (below) is a model of consistent appreciation. If we add in the dividend, the stock has, roughly speaking, kept pace with the general market if not slightly outperformed it.

What investors want to know is, what is the likelihood of this more recent trend continuing? Do shares represent good value with a positive risk-reward ratio at their current price? To answer these questions, we’ll delve into their pipeline, potential catalysts over the next year or so, and evaluate the company’s financials and valuation in more detail. There is also some recent news to discuss.

Pipeline and Catalysts

On September 17, the company brought out two press releases. The first was negative: they were discontinuing the trial of Fasenra for COPD after it failed to meet the primary endpoint. The drug is already approved for the treatment of asthma in more than 80 countries, and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis in adults in more than 60 countries. While this is disappointing news, it is not game-changing for the overall thesis of the company or even the drug itself. Sales of Fasenra for the approved indications mentioned above represented just 3.3% of first half 2025 total revenue for the company. Moreover, this trial was just 1 of 12 ongoing studies that AstraZeneca was expecting results from in 2H 2025.

Also released the same day was news that Saphnelo "demonstrated a statistically significant and clinically meaningful reduction in disease activity compared to placebo" for treating systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) through subcutaneous injection. The drug was proven both safe and efficacious. This is obviously positive news as the disease affects over 3.4 million people globally. Saphnelo generated $304 million last year, through its IV-based treatment in moderate to severe SLE, and saw a 49% revenue growth. The benefits of subcutaneous treatment in general – as outlined here and here – are lower infection risk, simplified administration (at home and by the patient themselves), and therefore improved convenience and some cost savings.

While the company’s drug pipeline is extensive and holds several promising treatments, prospective investors can never be sure what treatments will truly move the needle, as this is an incredibly dynamic space and there are so many other factors to consider, not least of all what other companies are doing behind the scenes to target the same indications. So, for pharmaceutical companies that offer such a large array of products, what I tend to do is take a step back from the current or prospective sales figures for individual products and instead focus on how the company is managing itself, specifically: investments, cost savings, management effectiveness, and track record. Some of these might be considered subjective, qualitative measures, but they are important.

Their pipeline is their main focus for investment and clearly extensive; the company is anticipating several catalysts in the form of trial data and approvals being sought in various locales globally. Their track record here is excellent. In the six months prior to reporting 1H 2025 results, they saw 19 approvals in key regions (U.S., EU, China and Japan) and have plans to deliver at least 20 NMEs (new molecular entities) by 2030 and $80 billion in total revenue. From their figure of $54 billion in 2024 revenue, this would represent a CAGR of about 6.8%. On top of this, they aim to continue their track record of reducing S,G&A spend relative to revenue, thereby anticipating an operating margin around the mid-30s (currently 25.4%).

Doing so would allow the company to divert more of its revenue toward both R&D and M&A, driving further revenue growth into the future. The company is a serial acquirer, having purchased at least 20 smaller companies since its founding—the most recent was EsoBiotec in May 2025. Generally speaking, management has proved itself adept in its acquisitions, which should give shareholders confidence going forward.

While a 6.8% forward revenue growth rate does not necessarily scream at us, their efforts to improve operating efficiencies in combination with their strong product portfolio and rich pipeline commands attention, and any revenue accretion through M&A activity should enhance this figure still further. EPS should continue to grow and, in combination with a healthy dividend (current yield 2% based on a 70% payout ratio and near 16% FCF margin), shareholders should be richly rewarded. Let’s take a deeper look at its financials and present valuation.

Financials and Valuation

AstraZeneca has cash and equivalents of about $7.11 billion, and total debt of $32.9 billion (just over $2 billion in short-term debt). It has total assets valued at $112 billion, which includes $21 billion in goodwill on the balance sheet. Total liabilities come to $67.6 billion, giving an assets/liabilities ratio of 1.65. Even stripping away the goodwill, the figure is 1.34.

The company has a lower quick and current ratio than I tend to find comfortable as an investor, but because it generates a lot of FCF and can fairly easily pay off its debt I am less concerned. Low numbers here would be most worrisome in pre-profit companies or those spending aggressively on growth in an unproven market, but this is not AstraZeneca. Their strong business model and FCF generation ($11.2 billion in the last twelve months) mitigates a lot of the leverage risk here. Its debt/equity ratio is healthy for a company of its size, and with interest rates falling globally, this should be less of a burden as interest expenses decline slightly.

In terms of its investments and uses of cash, the company has become increasingly efficient, seen in its rapidly improving returns since 2021 (below). This is an excellent sign.

In terms of valuation, the company trades at a forward P/E of 21.4 on a non-GAAP basis. This is 13.5% below the sector median and 31% below its historical average of nearly 31. Based on consensus estimates or earnings growth, it has a current (GAAP) PEG ratio of 1.

Seeking Alpha currently give it a C- grade for valuation, but it is clear from its P/S and Fwd P/E ratios that the company has become cheaper (in terms of its earnings multiple) over time, despite its steadily improving share price. This is also reflected in its increasing earnings and FCF yields since 2020.

Taken together, I would consider the company fairly priced, if not slightly undervalued, given its sustained growth, track record of innovative treatment, and solid pipeline of new products and new indications going forward.

In terms of what price I’d be tempted to start a position here, I’d look at its technicals, which might be instructive here. In its 5-year chart, the stock is clearly in a broad uptrend, about halfway between its upper and lower channels at present. I would be tempted to start a position if the share price fell a bit further (below $70), but only because I believe the company will sustain its consistent growth over the next few years and may possibly bounce off that trend line soon. I’m be willing to be patient with this one, knowing full well that I may miss the boat. That is the risk you take as a bargain stock hunter.

Conclusion

I believe AstraZeneca to be a well-managed, solid growth compounder. It has a superb track record, a wide moat in the industry, especially oncology, and has improved its operating margins through careful investments and business growth. I see no reason why management can’t sustain the wonderful progress they have made, and with their strong pipeline and numerous catalysts for the rest of 2025 and into 2026, I anticipate decent price appreciation. Will it beat the market over the net five or ten years? I’d probably give that a 50/50 chance, but with a possible recession looming alongside a stagflationary situation in the general economy, investors might rotate into these types of stocks—that is, those non-cyclical companies with strong balance sheets, decent dividends, and a track record of winning through difficult periods in the past.

I would urge investors to keep an eye on developments in China, given some regulatory concerns and recent investigations by the Chinese authorities at the end of last year. Some of these are still ongoing, and should be looked into by current and prospective shareholders. Revenue from China represented 12% of its total in 2024.

For shareholders looking for a long-term lower-risk equity, I would give this a Buy rating. If the NYSE ADR shares drop meaningfully below $70, I’d make it a strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Seix High Yield Bond SMA Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The second quarter of 2025 was marked by a dramatic reversal in sentiment, driven by shifting expectations around trade policy, inflation, and monetary policy. After a turbulent start to the quarter triggered by the April 2 “Liberation Day” announcements, markets stabilized and rallied into quarter-end, buoyed by easing inflation data, resilient corporate fundamentals, and renewed investor appetite for risk.

BB and single-B rated bonds performed largely in line with each other during the second quarter. The ICE BofA BB U.S. High Yield Index (H0A1) increased by 3.46%, while the ICE BofA Single-B U.S. High Yield Index (H0A2) increased by 3.47%. CCCs outperformed in 2Q25, reversing what we saw during the first quarter as the ICE BofA CCC & Lower U.S. High Yield Index (H0A3) increased by 4.28%. However, for the year-to-date period, BBs outperformed single-Bs and CCCs. The H0A1 index increased by 4.96% for the first half of the year, the H0A2 index increased by 4.20%, and the H0A3 index increased by 3.58%. BBBs, as measured by the ICE BofA BBB U.S. Corporate Index (C0A4), increased by 4.29% for the first half of the year.

The option-adjusted spread for the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index (H0A0) began the year at 292 basis points (bps), widened to 461 bps on April 7 shortly after the Liberation Day tariff announcements, and ended the quarter at 296 bps—almost back to where we began the year.

Performance

The Seix High Yield Bond SMA that we manage increased by 2.62% (gross) in 2Q25 (1.99% net). This performance was 95 bps fewer than the benchmark, the ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Cash Pay Index (J0A0), which increased by 3.57%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included cable satellite, gaming & leisure, and restaurants/food/beverage/supermarkets. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included healthcare/pharmaceuticals, retailers, and transportation/ automotive/aerospace.

The portfolio increased by 4.20% (gross) for the year-to-date period (2.91% net). This performance was 37 bps fewer than the benchmark, ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Cash Pay Index (J0A0), which increased by 4.57%. The top contributing sectors to relative performance included cable satellite, chemicals, and gaming & leisure. The bottom contributing sectors to relative performance included healthcare/pharmaceuticals, telecommunications, and hotels & REITs.

Our longer-term objective is to provide a smoother ride with outperformance through the full credit cycle and the strongest relative performance generated during periods of dislocation when the market is offering a plethora of attractively-priced securities.

Current Strategy & Outlook

As we enter the second half of 2025, our strategy remains grounded in disciplined credit analysis and a focus on the higher-quality segments of the high yield market. The macroeconomic backdrop continues to be defined by elevated policy uncertainty, slowing growth tempered by economic resilience, and sticky inflation. These conditions, combined with valuations that are at the tighter end of their historical range, reinforce our up-in-quality bias. This is further supported by the relative strength of BB-rated issuers, which have demonstrated more stable earnings and greater financial flexibility than CCC-rated issuers, which remain under pressure, with compressed margins and more constrained access to the capital markets. As a result, we remain underweight CCCs.

While fundamentals have begun to show signs of modest erosion, we believe the overall credit quality of the high yield market remains strong. The recent surge in refinancing activity, combined with ample liquidity and muted near-term maturity walls, has helped to push out maturities, which can be helpful in lowering default risk. Leverage for high yield issuers rose to 4.08x in 1Q, though this is still below the long-term average of 4.29x. Interest coverage declined modestly to 4.70x but remains well above the historical median, suggesting high yield issuers have above-average levels of financial flexibility. The increase in leverage and decline in interest coverage was due in part to declining profit margins, which have fallen to a four-year low. These trends reflect a market that is fundamentally sound but increasingly vulnerable to macro headwinds, particularly in lower-rated cohorts.

From a rating perspective, the trends have been less favorable this year. The year-to-date upgrade to downgrade ratio is at 0.88x to 1 (suggesting more downgrades than upgrades this year) compared to 1.22x for the same period in 2024. The upgrade to downgrade ratio was 0.54x in June, which was the second-worst month of the year (April being the worst). Looking beneath the surface, the year- to-date upgrade to downgrade ratios for BBs, single-Bs, and CCCs reveal that higher-quality assets show positive ratings momentum while lower-quality ones trend negatively. This pattern has remained consistent for at least the past year. Retail and consumer products experienced the greatest number of downgrades year-to-date.

From a valuation perspective, spreads remain tight by historical standards, but yields continue to offer attractive entry points, particularly in BBs and select single-Bs. Yields began the year just inside long-term median levels at 7.47%, widened to 8.65% (75th percentile), and ended the quarter at 7.05% (32nd percentile). The relatively attractive yields combined with solid fundamentals has supported trading levels for the higher-quality part of the market.

We remain constructive on high yield, but are maintaining a cautious and selective approach, favoring quality, liquidity, and flexibility as we navigate the remainder of the year. We believe this environment favors active management and security selection, with a focus on issuers that offer compelling risk-adjusted income and downside protection.

data.title: Crescent Capital: Time To Moderate My Expectations
data.description:
Crescent Capital BDC, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:CCAP) shareholders, including myself, faced CCAP's negative stock price performance in 2025. Since the beginning of the year, its stock price has dropped by over 18%. Luckily for us, that negative performance is somewhat offset by CCAP's attractive dividends.

Still, with decreasing interest rates (the Fed has just provided us with another 25 bps cut), many investors are left wondering what they can expect from CCAP and whether their dividends are sustainable. It's not surprising at all, especially given a BDC so susceptible to interest rate cuts. This a follow-up coverage to my previous article about CCAP, which I decided to prepare in light of new financial data available (indicating increasing problems with non-accruals) and the Fed's most recent decision. Let's review this.

CCAP's overview and its susceptibility to interest rate changes

CCAP is a debt-oriented BDC with high exposure to first lien debt, amounting to 91% of its portfolio. Having invested in nearly 190 companies (187 to be exact), CCAP's investments amount to $1.6B on a fair value basis.

I've mentioned above that CCAP is highly susceptible to interest rate cuts. It looks that way for CCAP, because 97% of its debt investments are structured with a floating rate. As a result of that, each interest rate cut has a negative impact on CCAP's investment income. To some degree, that's also the case for CCAP's own debt financing, but nearly 50% of its outstanding debt has a fixed-rate structure, so it's not like a reduced cost of debt is sufficient to offset reduced income potential.

Since the Fed started cutting interest rates around a year ago, we can clearly see the effects of that. Just look at the chart below and focus on CCAP's weighted average yield. It decreased from 12.2% in Q2 2024 to 10.4% in Q1 2025. It remained at the same level in Q2 2025 (as we didn't have any interest rate cuts in this period), but now - I'm afraid the weighted average yield will decline even further. Since we're already near the end of Q3 2025, the effects of the most recent interest rate cut will be truly visible in the data for Q4 2025.

These changes naturally translated into CCAP's ability to generate net investment income. When you look at its NII per share for the last couple of quarters, you'll easily notice that it dropped by ~22% in Q2 2025 when compared to Q2 2024. The regular dividend coverage, once showcasing a great 140% level, currently stands at ~10% and is set to decrease in the near term due to the last (and potentially upcoming) interest rate changes.

However, we can't look at interest rate changes only from the perspective of quantifiable metrics. There's one great benefit interest rate cuts that all BDCs share, regardless of their floating-rate debt exposure—improving the credit quality of their portfolio companies.

Just think about it. The Fed started increasing interest rates in mid-2022. It's been three years since then, and that's quite a lot of time for businesses to operate under a relatively high-interest rate environment (especially with such dynamic increases following a long period of low-interest rates). Many BDC management teams mentioned that businesses operating on the lower end of the liquidity profile were struggling with that.

The case isn't any different for CCAP, and we can easily see how that manifests in its portfolio, especially when reviewing Q1-Q2 2025 data. Look at the chart below presenting CCAP's internal performance ratings.

We can see two crucial changes:

  1. The share of 3-rated investments (performing below expectations) increased in Q4 2024–Q2 2025 when compared to Q2–Q3 2024.
  2. The share of 4/5-rated investments (performing materially/substantially below expectations) increased in Q2 2025 to 3%.

These are concerning changes, and they represent how much some businesses are struggling. CCAP's non-accruals have increased substantially to ~2.4% at fair value in Q2 2025 vs. 0.9% in Q4 2024. Still, it's crucial to remember that internal performance ratings serve as a monitoring list for the management, so it could stay proactive when it comes to managing any potential defaults.

As the management commented during the last Earnings Call:

It is worth noting that as of quarter-end, as a percentage of total investments at fair value, CCAP's watch list, which we define as 3, 4, and 5 rated investments, was 14%, whereas our nonaccruals at fair value were 2.4%, a nearly 12% gap. This is in contrast to an analysis of our public peers where this gap is approximately 6%. We believe this reflects our philosophy and our culture of being forward-looking in terms of maintaining our watch list. We do not, for example, wait until there is a default for moving something down the risk rating scale.

We have to know what to expect from CCAP

Last quarter, CCAP distributed $0.47 per share to its shareholders, which consisted of a regular dividend amounting to $0.42 per share and a special distribution amounting to $0.05 per share. As BDC investors, we care about income a lot. While the management claims that CCAP's regular dividend coverage is in excess of 10%, I wouldn't be as optimistic.

Excluding $0.02 per share of one-time accelerated amortization related to deferred financing costs, NII was $0.48 per share. Importantly, earnings remain in excess of our dividend with 110% base dividend coverage for the quarter.

Let me state right from the get-go - I don't think CCAP will be able to pay supplemental or special dividends for too long. Its total dividend coverage already nearly matches its NII (even after the one-time adjustment mentioned by the management), and the most recent interest rate cut has yet to have its effect on CCAP's income potential.

With just regular dividends taken into account, CCAP's coverage is likely to fall below 110%, the management mentioned by the end of this year. If the Fed continues its cutting journey, CCAP's coverage could even fall short of 100%.

Still, even when assuming that CCAP will only be able to pay regular dividends in the upcoming quarters, that's still quite an attractive yield of 10.7%. On top of that, CCAP trades at a ~20% discount to its NAV, so it's not like we're targeting an overvalued BDC.

Regardless, even though I've built a certain stake of shares in CCAP, I'm not eager to buy anymore. I respect its portfolio composition in terms of asset mix and its preventive approach to default management, but I haven't expected such material increases in non-accruals. When combined with the risk of losing the ability to maintain the current level of dividend payments, I'll be more comfortable with holding onto the stake I have and reinvesting dividends elsewhere.

Therefore, CCAP is a hold for me.

data.title: Beyond The Bill Worry: Using UTG For Sustainable Monthly Income
data.description:
As for passive income focused investor, Reaves Utility Income Trust (NYSE:UTG) has been one of my favorite AI-driven plays. In my previous article on UTG that was published March, this year, I emphasized the durability of the offered ~7% monthly yield as well as the high-probability event of benefiting from price appreciation already in the near-term.

Looking at the chart below, I would say that my thesis has worked out perfectly:

UTG has delivered even stronger returns than the S&P 500 (SPY), which as we know, has itself surprised many investors to the upside. And of course, UTG has clearly outperformed the broader energy complex (XLE) including income investors' go-to-choice - midstream sector (AMLP).

As a result of the strong performance, it is only logical that UTG's yield has deflated a bit, going from 7%+ when I issued my previous article to ~6.3% now. For some investors this might already be too low to dedicate a meaningful share of assets, as that would introduce a drag on the portfolio's overall yield level.

While I fully understand that ~6.3% yield is not necessarily the juiciest option in terms of speeding up the income compounding process, we have to keep in mind the following context:

  • Just recently, the Fed decreased the base rates, and the expectation is that there will be additional cuts in 2025. So, on a relative basis, UTG's yield is actually quite decent.
  • UTG is well-positioned to deliver incremental distribution hikes. For example, in June, 2025, after a long pause, UTG finally bumped up its dividend by 5.3%.
  • Given the structural dynamics on the inflation and AI CapEx-spend side, the story of UTG's outperformance has certainly not finished.

Let me flesh out these items in a bit more detail below.

Thesis review

Just a quick reminder for those, who have not followed UTG so closely.

UTG is a closed-end fund with a focus on utility, energy, and infrastructure assets. As most of CEFs, UTG employs external leverage to magnify the yield that stems from the underlying portfolio holdings. In addition, UTG has a clear bias towards high-quality and investment grade names, which supports both NAV and yield stability, and, importantly, it de-risks the external leverage component.

Now, the reason why UTG has managed to register so strong returns so far this year is mostly linked to the secular tailwinds for high-quality energy, utility, and power infrastructure. I would distinguish two specific aspects that feed into the tailwinds:

  1. The notion of AI gaining significant traction basically across almost every single value chain in the economy. The booming AI factor, per definition, requires a supporting infrastructure to accommodate data - i.e., the build-out of data centers, at a very large scale. Just think of the announced CapEx plans by, say, Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Microsoft Corporation (NASDAQ:MSFT). These data centers, in turn, have to be powered by reliable energy, which is where such sectors as multi utilities, electric utilities and independent power and renewable electricity producers come in (these three alone comprise ~70% of UTG's book).
  2. The inflation seems to remain higher for longer due to such structural factors as tariffs, de-globalization, and a weaker dollar. For businesses that UTG holds in its portfolio this is great news, as they can pass through the inflation to their customers through take-or-pay agreements or regulated cash flows (e.g., MLPs, power transmission and grid operators, energy producers that collect cash flows from long-term power purchase agreements etc.). In essence, the CPI factor can nicely enhance the organic growth for most UTG portfolio holdings.

Apart from the aforementioned tailwinds, there is another important driver that should support UTG's net investment income generation. It comes from having lower and lower base rates.

Currently, UTG carries an external leverage load of 17.5% as measured from the total asset base. It is actually a modest leverage level, as usually yield-focused CEFs such as PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:PDO), Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (NYSE:RNP) and NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund's (NYSE:NXG) utilize leverage of around 25% to even 40% of their total asset base. In other words, UTG has definitely a financial capacity to expand its portfolio without risking the credit risk profile.

Yet, the most direct impact from lower base rates manifests itself through cheaper debt service, which is inherently accommodative for UTG's net investment income generation. And the beauty here is that the FOMC dot plot indicates that the overall direction in the base rates is negatively sloped, meaning that as we go forward, there will be further support to UTG's cash flows from cheaper debt service.

Finally, what also comes in handy is the lack of a tangible premium, which, in my view, could be fully warranted given the aforementioned drivers. By buying UTG now, we have to pay only ~0.75% in excess to the current NAV, which is actually below the 52-week high of 4.4%.

The bottom line

As an income-oriented investor, I view UTG as a must-have asset in my portfolio. Granted, the yield is not the highest that we can get, but in the context of UTG's strengths, I see a fully justified case for the investment.

If I had to label UTG's offering, then it would be something along the lines of "inflation-linked and sleep well at night recurrent income machine." In essence, by buying UTG now, we get both worlds: 1) monthly cash flows, which are quite decent, and 2) value stability in terms of price and on an inflation-adjusted basis (e.g., to fund rising living costs).

It is still a buy from my side.

data.title: From Mag 7 To Great 8: Why Palantir Could Be The Next Trillion-Dollar Giant
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The “Magnificent 7” was a term coined back in 2023, as a select group of tech giants increased their influence over the economy and began to outperform almost every other stock in the S&P 500.

The list includes: Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA).

But what exactly does it take to be part of the “Magnificent” club?

In my opinion, Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) is making a strong case to be included in this select group of companies.

This ultimately supports the strong valuation that Palantir commands.

I’ve made a similar case before, but today we go even further by expanding into why the narrative around Palantir is so compelling, and also justifying the price with a simple 15-year FCF projection.

There are also a couple of key factors that make me a lot more bullish, even with the threat of China I discussed in my last piece.

What Defines the Magnificent 7

The Mag 7 aren’t just big tech companies. Investors need to understand that today’s world has changed a lot in the last 20 years.

Evidence of this can be found in the fact that President Donald Trump recently hosted some of the most prominent tech leaders at the White House.

Tech doesn’t just shape the economy. These companies shape culture and even influence politics.

So what exactly are the characteristics of these stocks? I would summarize them as follows

  • Scale: Revenues measured in the hundreds of billions, with usually a global footprint.

  • Dominant ecosystems: Platforms that lock in users and developers. They benefit from positive network externalities.

  • Profitability: Strong cash flow, high margins, and cash-filled balance sheets make these companies even more attractive.

  • Secular growth drivers: These companies all benefit in one way or another from secular growth in different areas of technology.

  • Utility like ubiquity: This is arguably the most important. Most of these companies are, in their own way, irreplaceable, and their services could even be seen as modern-day utilities.

Let’s go through the list.

Apple: They essentially command 50% of the smartphone market. At this point, it’s unfathomable to think of any grown adult living without one. Pair this with brand loyalty and Apple services, and you have a very strong customer lock-in factor.

Microsoft: The operating system of the majority of computers, and by extension of the most used business and intelligence software worldwide.

Amazon: It's hard to believe we once lived without one-day shipping. Amazon is expanding to new regions every day and becoming a powerhouse cloud too.

Alphabet: Everything on the Internet goes through Google, YouTube is huge, and now Alphabet seems to even be leading in AI.

Meta: Facebook may be old news, but Meta also has Instagram and WhatsApp. Literally over a billion people use Meta in one way or another.

Nvidia: Backbone of the AI revolution, to such an extent that its chips have become a geopolitical issue.

Tesla: Arguably the least 'magnificent' in my opinion right now. EV adoption has failed to live up to the hype. However, once you bundle in Tesla with CEO Elon Musk's portfolio (Twitter and SpaceX), clearly, the company holds a very important position, too.

These companies, and their founders/CEOs, stand head and shoulders above the rest of the market.

They are responsible for most of the earnings growth of the S&P 500.

And they're the infrastructure of the digital economy.

Palantir’s Unique Position

This idea, in my opinion, largely explains Palantir's meteoric rise. A lot of investors find it kind of unfathomable that Palantir can trade at such a high multiple.

50 years ago, this might have been an accurate assessment, but not today.

Today’s economy is built on the back of a few very large tech giants, and it’s likely going to keep going this way.

Yes, there will be healthy competition and plenty of companies that do well, but there will be one or two that, over the next decade, will join the pantheon of the tech giants.

The market has decided this is where Palantir belongs.

Palantir is built differently in three key ways

Government Stickiness

Over the last two years, Palantir has become an indispensable contractor to the U.S. government and some foreign governments, too.

In other words, Palantir could soon be the largest supplier of the largest employer in the world, the U.S. government.

It just stands to reason that Palantir is going to get much bigger, as it becomes more and more entrenched in the government.

Commercial Growth

Palantir's AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) is growing at a fast pace in the U.S., and is slowly gaining presence abroad.

AI could potentially hold the secret to unleashing a large-scale increase in efficiency.

Palantir is no doubt one of the largest beneficiaries of this trend, and its use could explode within companies.

Again, think about AIP becoming as common as Microsoft Office.

Profitability And Moat

The potential for cash generation is huge. Palantir is highly scalable, with very handsome margins. A gross profit margin of 80% could eventually translate into income margins comparable to Meta.

Why I'm More Bullish Despite China

But what has changed since my last report?

Back then, I highlighted that markets might be overlooking the risk that Palantir could become too big for its own good, so to speak, making it hard to grow internationally as it handles sensitive data.

This is something we have seen happen, for example, with Nvidia's chips.

But upon further examination, this risk may have been overstated.

For starters, if you actually look at the list of Mag 7 companies, they are all susceptible to Chinese/International boycotts. Apple and Tesla have production facilities in China, Meta gets around 11% of its revenue from China, and the same goes for the other big names.

This risk will be ever-present and is escalating, but it shouldn't be a reason not to invest.

But what makes me even more bullish now is the fact that Palantir just scored a landmark deal with the UK of close to $1 billion.

This is pretty significant, because the UK and Palantir already had some issues as the company took on an NHS contract.

This contract, however, opens the door to the UK government, and potentially the commercial sector too.

This potentially signifies a change and could still provide Palantir with decades of growth as the doors open to more US allies.

Even though there will be hurdles to worldwide expansion, there's a clear road to go much, much higher from here in terms of TAM and growth.

Playing The Long Game

Palantir has the qualities of a Mag 7, and it is also experiencing similar growth. Cash flow has exploded in the last two years, and this will likely continue.

For reference, let’s imagine Palantir's next 15 years look similar to Meta’s first 15 years in public markets.

During this time, Meta delivered an FCF growth of over 25%.

If Palantir could do this over the next 15 years, then we’d be looking at over $32 billion in FCF in 15 years.

At 20x FCF, here’s what the share price could look like in 15 years. 2.37

Conservative (18% CAGR FCF): ~$124/share

Meta-like (25% CAGR FCF): ~$293/share

Aggressive (32% CAGR FCF): ~$665/share

So, is Palantir really that overpriced?

With Meta, like FCF growth of 25% sustained YoY growth, the annualized return over the next 15 years is just shy of 4%.

At the more aggressive $665 price target, that’s 9.5% per year, market-beating returns.

Final Thoughts

Here’s the bottom line.

Palantir is expensive for a reason, and that’s a very good reason.

I don’t think there’s a single company out there today that has a better chance of making it than Palantir.

And by making it, I mean becoming a tech giant on par with the Magnificent 7

There’s a risk that Palantir could become too big, so to speak, and face backlash internationally.

However, simply becoming the main partner of the U.S. government is enough to justify aggressive growth over the coming decade and beyond.

Ultimately, Palantir has changed the name of the game in data, and all evidence points to the fact that it will become a tech giant.

The price today is high, but reasonable if we assume growth on par with Mag 7 companies in the early days.

data.title: Brodrick And Eade On What's Really Powering The Rally In Stocks And Metals
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Sean Brodrick of Weiss Ratings and John Eade of Argus Research unpack today’s market drivers, record equities, gold’s strongest year since 1979, and ongoing Federal Reserve interest rate cuts.

Transcript

Mike Larson: It's a bonanza out there in the markets. If by markets, you mean groups like precious metals and mining stocks, AI names, and certain up and coming sectors like financials, and that more diverse list of winners speaks to an important point. Unlike early on in this bull run, you can hit pay dirt in more places than just big tech. Even better, my two guests this week say the party isn't over yet. They also come bearing gifts, stocks they think you can still buy now for more potential profits. So let's get to it. My guests are Sean Brodrick, Senior Editor at Weiss Ratings, and John Eade, Chairman and CEO of Argus Research.

Gentlemen, thank you both for taking time out to chat on the MoneyMasters Podcast. Well, Sean, John, I really appreciate you both taking some time out to talk. I know we've got the Orlando event coming up. Really excited about seeing you both in person there soon. Let's talk about the big picture here. I mean, we've had this relentless equity market rally. Gold's having its best year since 1979. We have all this political and sort of geopolitical chaos, and yet it doesn't really seem to be impacting markets. So I kind of want to start there and then drill down. I guess, Sean, we'll start with you. What do you see big picture?

Sean Brodrick: Well, many people confuse the economy and the stock market. They're really separate things. The stock market's more of a wealth index and corporations. The big ones anyway are actually doing fine, so the market should keep going higher.

At the same time, there are some things that are tailwinds to the market. For example, the U.S. dollar has lost over 10% of its value this year, right? And so, as the value of the dollar goes down, things that are priced in the dollar tend to go up, that includes stocks. So there's a bunch of things that will help the market, turns out that the tariffs weren't as big of an obstacle as many people, including me, first thought, or at least the impact of that is spread out over time, so it's easier to deal with.

And there are areas of the market which are quite strong. I'm -- one, I think is precious metals, materials, stuff like that. We're doing extraordinarily well. I think we'll continue to do well on that. AI, of course, has been the big lift for the broad market. We'll see how long that continues, but there's plenty of good things like in this market. So people shouldn't be too pessimistic, I think.

Mike Larson: Fair enough. John, how about you? I mean, when you came into this year, you were probably kicking around some ideas for how Argus felt the markets would be. I mean, do you think we'd see this kind of strength again after two plus 20-some odd percent years for the S&P?

John Eade: So, Mike, thanks for having me on. Glad to be here. And the answer is, yes. We feel at Argus that the stock market, which Sean pointed out, the S&P 500, the wealth of the economy is in a bull market. The bull market started in October 2022. As you know, over the many cycles, there are bull markets; there are bear markets. But your average bull market lasts a little bit over five years. So we're coming into about three years into a bull market, and your average bull market stocks rise about 150%, and we're up about 80%. So I can't call it the early innings of a bull market, but the fundamentals are intact to keep this bull market going.

And by those fundamentals, I mean GDP. We've avoided a recession so far in 2025. I think we're going to make it through the back half of the year without a recession and through 2026 as well. The economy is growing. Not perfectly, but it's growing.

Corporate profits are doing better, right? Corporate profits are growing at a double-digit pace. You're getting top line growth, and because the tech sector is now such a big part of the overall market and the tech sector's margins are generally higher than industrials or banks or consumer companies, you're getting higher profit margins as well.

The third factor that's working into all of this is that interest rates are coming down. I think the Fed is going to cut rates tomorrow and then cut rates two more times for the balance of the year. And the 10-year was up close to 5% earlier this year. It's now down to 4%, so the fundamentals are intact. The bull market is not too old. It's not cheap. I can't tell you that the dirt bargains here, but there are enough fundamentals to keep us optimistic looking ahead into 2026.

Mike Larson: Got it. Yeah. We're recording this the day before the Fed's meeting announcement, and it'll be released the day after. So let's assume that a cut has happened by the time this airs. You said a couple more for this year. I want to know, and I don't want to talk too much about macro, I want to talk about tech, though, since you brought it up. The AI bubble, boom, what is it? Is it sustainable? I've been asked that. I've had people ask it at our events and so on. So since you brought up tech, what are your thoughts on that? I mean, is this sustainable? Is this something we need to worry about? At what point do we worry? I guess that's what would be my question there. And I'll start with you, John, then maybe go to you, Sean.

John Eade: Sure. Well, we had a worry earlier this year. If you remember that we had the competing AI solution out of China, and that sent stocks on their way to a bear market, never quite got there, because I think investors realized there's more to AI than just the raw power. You have to be able to access it across geopolitical situations. You have to be able to connect it into existing infrastructure. And these U.S. companies may not have the cheapest mousetrap, but it's working real well now, and we think likely to continue driving earnings for a while.

So I wouldn't say the tech is a bubble, although it's up to over 30% of the market, but the earnings growth has been real strong. It's been leading the market in earnings too as well as in stock market returns. So I'm okay with tech now. And, Mike, maybe I'll come back later and talk about how we're excited about seeing tech show up in other sectors too.

Mike Larson: Okay. Great. Sean, big picture. Your thoughts on that, that sort of AI debate?

Sean Brodrick: Sure. Well, we've made a lot of money in, like, AI this year in Supercycle Investor. We've sold out of most of our strictly AI stocks. What we have a bunch of now are what you call high-tech defense stocks and things like drones and stuff like that, a lot of which uses AI. I mean that's really making the next leap for these weapons of the future is the fact they can start to think for themselves, which is terrifying if you're on the receiving end. But I mean, if you don't have to send your soldiers into harm's way, you can see how popular these weapons are. So, we're doing well with that.

We also have building out the power grid because the more AI we use, then the more power we need. So the power grid has to be built out, but that's kind of slow growth really. So really, what we're looking at now is ways to leverage AI in other areas like defense, stuff like that. Because there's the next wave that is coming of , which is really quite exciting.

Mike Larson: Okay. And I definitely want to get down to some of these picks here from both of you. But before I do, Sean, I have to ask you, you were just at the Precious Metals Summit in Beaver Creek, Colorado. I imagine it was probably a little bit more of a light-hearted affair this year than it has been in years past. Talk about the environment there and just what people had to say at that event.

Sean Brodrick: People were so freaking happy. I mean, mining is a long-term proposition anyway. So the guys who are in this business have been through both boom times and downtimes, often the older ones, multiple cycles. And for example, I had a great talk with Rob McEwen, who's a mining legend. He brought -- I mean, that guy has built mining companies before; he's doing it now. He thinks there's a great environment for it. He thinks we aren't even close to the end of the cycle. I agree. I mean, gold, when it gets in a bull market, tends to run for years. We're two years into this one. We probably have at least five more to go.

And even though the -- like stocks have been playing catch up this year quite a bit, technically speaking, they're still undervalued because where they were last valued, gold was at least $500 cheaper, and so now it's just getting much, much higher. So, there's a lot of room to run, and gold stocks are underowned by the broad market. I saw some number -- I think it was today, like, only 5% of investors have exposure to any kind of precious metal stocks or anything like that. In a big bull market, that's going to change.

So we're still in the early stages. There are some early stage stocks, which I think are really undervalued. I'm about to send out lists to my subscribers, so I'd rather not talk about them too much. But I mean, even if you do something like buy the GDXJ, which is the Gold Junior Miner ETF, that's up 112% so far this year alone since the beginning of 2025. A lot of people would tell you that's overbought, and I'd agree normally. And I expect a pullback. I'd love to see a pullback in that. I just think it's going a lot higher.

Mike Larson: Got it. And we're going to come back to you. I might have to hold your feet over the fire for maybe just one name you don't mind throwing out. But John, I want to go to you. You talked about how some of -- one of your themes is other sectors that are benefiting from AI. Is there a sector you want to talk about or a name or two you want to throw out there that you think is really putting this technology to use in kind of an innovative and highly profitable way?

John Eade: Sure, Mike. Let's talk about the financial services sector to start, okay? So this is a long time sleepy sector, with insurance stocks and big banks and regional stocks. Now a few years ago, S&P and GICS jumbled things up. They split off the real estate section. They jammed in the payment processors, like, Mastercard and Visa. And boom, now you've got a real business model change in financial services. It's not just spreads anymore. It's not just deposits versus loan rates. It's not just premiums versus payments. Now you've got transactions, right? So, you've got stocks like Visa (V), like Mastercard (MA), other consumer finance companies like American Express (AXP). The exchanges have taken a central role as a lot of risks have emerged around the world.

So there are new products on exchanges like NASDAQ, Cboe and the commodities exchange. And then you've got the data companies too, right? There are stock pickers, sure. But passive investing has been a prominent investment trend these past few years, and that's all built on indexes created by places like Argus, but also, of course, like S&P, Moody's and MSCI.

So tech is really starting to infiltrate into financial services too, and that's always been a value sector financial services. The P/Es are getting a little bit richer, but the earnings are getting a little bit more robust, or maybe I'll say a lot more robust. And I think there's opportunity left in financials, particularly going back to the spreads with the Fed about to cut rates.

Mike Larson: Got it. And since Sean mentioned aerospace and defense, I know that was a sector you had in your most weekly update that you highlighted there, John. Any names or particular companies there or within that space that you like the most?

John Eade: So I'd be focusing more on the aerospace here. A couple popped to mind. One is GE Aerospace (GE). It seems like they've finally gotten that management structure and management strategy correct by getting rid of the health care business. And now they've also, of course, divested the power business, which is doing very well. But aerospace on its own is getting back up to more normal industry average margins. The orders are coming in. And then one of their suppliers also is an interesting idea, Howmet Aerospace (HWM), the old part of Alcoa and Arconic. This is like engineered products, truck wheels, and airplane parts. But that's been a good growth opportunity as well.

Mike Larson: Okay. Sean, in that mining sector, I mean, again, I know some of the smaller names, whatever that's you got to be good for your subscribers, and I understand that. Is there anybody maybe at the mid or high end of the tier in terms of market cap or diversified base and so on with their minds that you think you don't mind sharing here? And if so, what in particular about that name might stand out?

Sean Brodrick: Well, let me tell you this. You can buy the larger stocks, and you probably do well. I mean, we have Newmont Mining (NEM), for example. And I just heard from Barrick today, they have a new mine out in Nevada that they just think is going to be the next things since sliced bread. So the big ones will go up, but the real opportunity is in the smaller mines. Just to give you an example, we have Alamos Gold (AGI). I think we have -- we're up, like, 70% in that. That's going much higher.

We also have smaller ones like Heliostar Metals (OTCQX:HSTXF), which you have to go down to the OTC for that. And I know the OTC -- now this is an OTCQX stock, which is the best of the OTC. The OTC scares people, but I think that if you're on the OTCQX, stuff like that, Heliostar, you can do extraordinarily well. And when you look at how much that company is planning on increasing its mining output over the next few years, that's something that a big miner just can't do. I mean, a big miner can increase its output. But it's not going to go from like, 50,000 to 100,000 to much higher than that. And so that's why I think people should look at the smaller stocks because they will really get some nice surprises there.

Mike Larson: Alright. How about in the material space, any of the outside of precious metals? And this would kind of be for both of you since, especially, John, you've talked about tangential AI plays. I've had a lot of people throw out copper and, how much copper is being used in building up the grid and other parts of this sort of AI infrastructure. What are your thoughts on base metals or copper plays? Anything you like there?

John Eade: So I'll step in there for a second, Mike. Yeah. The copper is certainly a great story. It's a great metal. It's lightweight. It's super conductive. It has enormous applications in energy conservation and the green economy. The prices bounce around. It's really hard to trade. So, in our opinion, buy a stock like Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) and put it away for two or three years. Otherwise, you are going to be buying and selling every other day or every other week.

A real, even deeper value than copper, I think, would be lithium, which is still down about 90% from its prices six or seven years ago. And it's gotten so cheap that some of the big companies like Rio Tinto (RIO) have started to acquire lithium assets, as they look to diversify their portfolio of metals down the road.

But the more pure-play lithium company, and it's risky as I'll get out, but it is Albemarle (ALB). It's almost exclusively linked to prices of lithium, so the stock is way down. But management, I'm impressed, they're focusing on controlling what they can control, and that is pulling back on CapEx a little bit when the environment isn't so promising; cutting back on their expenses. They're going to be cash flow positive this year. And let's say lithium prices double. There's still going to be 70% down from all-time highs. They’re still going to be cheap, but that's going to be a lot of extra cash running through Albemarle.

Mike Larson: Great. Great ideas there. Sean, anything you want to add? I know you've talked about lithium market before and other – and copper for that matter.

Sean Brodrick: Sure. Well, first, I want to congratulate you on saying Albemarle, right? Because every time I try and say that, I screw it up. So good for you.

Now, I just took profits, nice profits on a lithium stock, so I won't mention that. We are doing extremely well in antimony or as my Australian's friend say, antimony, whichever way you say that, and rare earths. We're doing well in both of those and also uranium. I mean, people have to look at uranium now. It's really becoming part of the energy solution for pretty much everyone. And all of these three things that I just mentioned, antimony, rare earths and there's all kinds of rare earths, but we'll just use that group, and also uranium, these are all getting support from the US government. And it's nice when Uncle Sam keeps putting money in your pocket. And that's worked out just to boost those stocks like you wouldn't believe.

So, you can look at all those -- but I will give you a copper name. We're only up about 30% of it, and so why not? The other ones I just mentioned, we have triple-digit gains. So people say, oh, he's just full of it. He's just talking about that.

But the copper stock isn't that up much yet, but it's going to be. It's called Taseko Mines, symbol TGB. Now they have a new project coming online, which is just going to be fantastic. So you're still early stage in that. I mean, it has a long way to go. We also own Freeport, by the way, which is a nice pick that's in the monthly newsletter, but we tend to pick larger stocks for that. If you want to move down the scale, look at a smaller company, look at one with some extraordinary potential in the next few years. If you're a little patient, Taseko would be the way to go.

Mike Larson: Again, jeez, I asked for one or two names, and I feel like I've hit the jackpot here. So I appreciate both of you being so forthcoming. As we get to the tail end of our time here, obviously, we're really excited you're both going to be joining us for the October MoneyShow, the Orlando MoneyShow that's October 16 to 18. Sean, I know your topic is Chaos Kings, what you should buy in gold and silver now. And, John, your topic is how long can this bull market go. We've sort of touched on those, I guess. And, Sean, yours is pretty self-explanatory, but just give the 30-second view. Somebody comes to see you, what are they going to get out of your presentation? Sean, I'll start with you first.

Sean Brodrick: Alright. Well, anyone who comes to see me, if they did not buy what I recommended at the last time I spoke at the MoneyShow, they have immediate regret because all those gold names have gone through the roof. So I have a whole bunch of new gold names for people, many of which come from Beaver Creek, so it's going to be great. Many of these are on the launchpad. And, yes, there is risk in everything, but we are in a big bull market for gold. So while there's risk, the upside reward potential is just enormous. So keep that in mind.

Mike Larson: Fair enough. John, same to you. I mean, again, how long can this bull market go? You sort of teased that at the outset, but, anything else that people are going to get some nuggets they might walk away from when they watch you in Orlando?

John Eade: Yeah. Well, Mike, you know that there are going to be ups and downs in the economy. There's going to be geopolitical and political developments, and those are going to scare some people. I'm going to try to get them to focus on some of the clear messages that come from company managements about how their businesses are doing. Which companies are raising guidance? Which companies are buying back a lot of stock? Which companies are increasing the dividends at a 10% pace every year when the rest of the market is increasing dividends maybe 2% or 3%? These are the confident companies that are going to navigate any of these challenges that will get in the later innings of the bull market. And those are the stocks I'm going to be trying to focus the MoneyShow participants on.

Mike Larson: Perfect. Sean, John, thank you so much for your time.

Sean Brodrick: Thanks for having us.

John Eade: Thanks, Mike.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Portfolio Review

The Silvant Large Cap Growth SMA (MUTF:STCAX) returned +18.62% (gross)/+17.74% (net) for the quarter, outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index’s return of +17.84%. Stock selection in consumer discretionary and industrials and an underweight and stock selection in consumer staples benefited performance. Stock selection in healthcare weighed on returns.

NVIDIA (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL) were among the top stock contributors to relative performance.

  • Artificial intelligence (AI) chipmaker NVIDIA sharply rebounded, fueled by a beat-and-raise quarter with 69% revenue growth and continued leadership in AI processing. While near-term uncertainty around China-related sales persisted, demand for its new Blackwell GPUs remained exceptionally strong. We added to the position based on the company’s upbeat outlook and positive key metrics.
  • Despite experiencing elevated volatility, Apple contributed positively to performance due to the Strategy’s underweight exposure, having previously held the stock at overweight levels. The recent quarter showed modest beats in revenue and units, but signs of channel fill ahead of potential tariff challenges and a lackluster AI strategy have tempered our outlook on the stock, although it remained a top holding.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and Eli Lilly (LLY) were among the largest stock detractors from relative performance.

  • Health insurer UnitedHealth Group fell following a weak earnings and revenue report, despite a better-than-expected key metric membership loss ratio. Elevated care utilization, particularly in its Medicare Advantage business, and challenges in the Optum segment pressured results; the CEO subsequently resigned and the company withdrew its 2025 guidance. Due to these execution issues, we sold the position, having previously reduced it in December.
  • Pharmaceutical firm Eli Lilly lagged despite strong earnings and positive Phase III data for its oral GLP-1 Orforglipron, targeting type 2 diabetes and weight loss. The weakness stemmed from CVS’s selection of competitor Wegovy as its preferred obesity drug, raising concerns about pricing pressure and other exclusive pharmacy benefit manager deals. We held steady with the stock, as the company’s 2025 guidance already factored in pricing declines, and its superior Zepbound plus Orforglipron’s launch should help secure its leadership in the $100 billion-plus obesity market.

Portfolio Changes

New positions included Boston Scientific (BSX) and Cloudflare (NET). In addition to UnitedHealth (discussed above), exits included Colgate-Palmolive (CL).

  • New holding Boston Scientific brings a diversified, high-growth medical device portfolio, with strong performance across key metrics, including double-digit organic growth, successful product launches, pipeline advancement, and margin expansion.
  • We bought web infrastructure and security provider Cloudflare, attracted by its expanding enterprise-focused platform and competitive advantages in AI-driven cloud and edge computing. As an AI cloud leader, it also is well positioned to gain market share and cross-sell a broad suite of products to its growing client base.
  • We sold our long-held position in consumer products company Colgate-Palmolive in favor of higher-conviction ideas, as the business faces increasing volume pressures tied to growing consumer price sensitivity.

Outlook

At this stage of the cycle, one of the primary questions is whether inflation from tariffs will slow growth materially this year. While these effects take time to work through, we currently do not expect a slowdown severe enough to trigger a recession. Some drag on growth is likely, but the extent remains uncertain. Earnings continue to beat expectations, especially in large-cap tech and communication services, where estimate revisions have driven strong gains. Second quarter GDP looks to be around 2.5%, rebounding from first quarter softness, and while second-half estimates are being trimmed, they remain positive. Additionally, the recent “Big Beautiful Bill” tax and spending bill adds tax cut and debt ceiling clarity, removing some policy overhang, though it may create fiscal headwinds due to its deficit impact. While we remain mindful of these macro risks, particularly inflation and Federal Reserve policy, we maintain our bottom-up focus and continue to find opportunities in fundamentally strong companies, especially in AI-related areas of tech and communication services, where growth continues to be attractive.
