Silvant Capital Management LLC claims compliance with the Global Investment Performance Standards (GIPS®) and has prepared and presented this report in compliance with the GIPS standards. Silvant Capital Management LLC has been independently verified for the periods of March 31, 2008 (the date of the Firm’s founding) through December 31, 2023. The verification reports are available upon request. A firm that claims compliance with the GIPS standards must establish policies and procedures for complying with all the applicable requirements of the GIPS standards. Verification provides assurance on whether the firm’s policies and procedures related to composite and pooled fund maintenance, as well as the calculation, presentation, and distribution of performance, have been designed in compliance with the GIPS standards and have been implemented on a firm-wide basis. Verification does not provide assurance on the accuracy of any specific performance report.

2. Definition of the Firm. Silvant Capital Management LLC (“Silvant”) is an SEC- registered investment adviser and is a wholly owned subsidiary of Virtus Partners, Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of Virtus Investment Partners, Inc. (“Virtus”). Registration of an investment adviser does not imply any level of skill or training. Silvant manages assets for segregated accounts and pooled investment vehicles. Silvant became an affiliate of Virtus effective June 1, 2017, when Virtus acquired RidgeWorth Capital Management LLC and RidgeWorth Investments (“RidgeWorth”). Silvant was created March 31, 2008, when all the investment decision-makers associated with the growth equity investment strategies of RidgeWorth became employees of Silvant. The staff and decision- making process remained intact and independent within Silvant. For composite reporting purposes, the Firm is defined as all equity portfolios managed by Silvant. Composite performance results are linked to performance history generated at RidgeWorth.

3. Composite Description. The Large Cap Growth Wrap Strategy Composite (the “Composite”) consists of all Institutional Large Cap Growth accounts managed by Silvant and does not include any Wrap Fee/SMA portfolios. The Composite includes fully discretionary accounts managed in accordance with the large cap growth style, with the objective of investing in in stocks with a competitive advantage, strong profitability metrics, above average free cash flow, above average operating margins, and positive price trends. Under normal circumstances, portfolios within this Composite will have at least 80% of their assets invested in common stocks and other U.S. traded equity securities of large cap companies. U.S. traded equity securities may include American Depository Receipts (“ADR’s”). Silvant considers large cap companies to be companies with market capitalizations similar to those of companies in the Russell 1000 Growth Index. Silvant will seek out securities it believes have strong business fundamentals, such as revenue growth, improving cash flows, increasing margins and positive earnings trends. Key material risks: Stock prices will decline, and the composite will underperform its benchmark. Effective January 1, 2025, the minimum portfolio size for the Large Cap Growth Wrap Strategy Composite is $100,000. Prior to 1/1/2025, the minimum portfolio size for the Composite was $1,000,000.

4. Benchmark. The benchmark for the Large Cap Growth Wrap Strategy Composite is the Russell 1000 Growth Index, which measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. Index returns do not reflect the deduction of any fees or expenses.

5. Returns and Fees. Returns are calculated based on total return which (a) includes cash returns, realized and unrealized gains plus income, and reinvestment of dividends and other earnings; (b) utilizes trade date and accrual accounting; and (c) is after the deduction of actual trading fees and expenses. Portfolio returns are calculated utilizing daily valuation. Composite returns, calculated monthly, are the weighted average return of the underlying portfolios using beginning-of- period market values. Composite gross of fee returns do not reflect any deduction for investment advisory fees, custodial charges, or other costs that a client might incur in connection with the management of an account. Investment advisory fees are described in Part 2 of the firm’s Form ADV. Prior to September 1, 2015, the gross of fee return for the mutual fund was calculated by adding back the fund’s published total operating expense ratio to the net of fee mutual fund performance; we now use the gross of fee return of the underlying portfolio. Net returns are calculated using the wrap fee, which includes all charges for portfolio management, custody and other administrative fees and are calculated by subtracting the highest applicable wrap fee (3.0% on an annual basis, or .25% monthly), on a monthly basis from the gross composite monthly return. Individual results may vary due to market conditions, trading costs, account-specific factors such as varying account minimums, and other unique circumstances.

6. Fee Schedule. The investment management fee schedule for the wrap programs vary between 1.25% and 3.00%. Bundled fees may vary by Sponsor. The client is referred to the Program Sponsor’s ADV 2A for a full disclosure of the fee schedule.

7. Ex Post Standard Deviation. The three-year annualized standard deviation measures the variability of the composite gross returns, and the benchmark returns over the preceding 36-month period. No three-year annualized standard deviations are reported for periods less than 3 years (shown as N/A).

8. Composite Dispersion. The dispersion of annual or YTD returns is measured by the equal-weighted standard deviation of portfolio gross returns represented within the composite for the full year or period. No dispersion is reported for periods with five or fewer portfolios (shown as N/A).

9. Currency. Valuations and returns are computed and stated in U.S. Dollars.

10. Composite Creation and Inception Date. This composite was created on January 1, 2025 and has performance history with an inception date of April 1, 2007.

11. Significant Cash Flow Policy. Beginning March 31, 2008, composite policy required the temporary removal of any portfolio incurring a client initiated significant cash inflow or outflow of 10% or greater of portfolio assets. This policy was discontinued effective January 1, 2025.

12. Availability of Information. Policies for valuing investments, calculating performance, and preparing GIPS Reports are available upon request. A list of all composite investment strategies offered by the firm, with a description of each strategy, is available upon request. The type of portfolios in which each strategy is available (segregated account, limited distribution pooled fund, or broad distribution pooled fund) is indicated in the description of each strategy.

13. Disclaimer. Past performance is not indicative of future results and no investment is guaranteed for return of principal and/or return on investments. Results will vary among accounts. All information provided and used in calculations is believed to be correct, but accuracy cannot be guaranteed. Please consult with a financial professional before investing.

14. Trademark. GIPS® is a registered trademark of CFA Institute. CFA Institute does not endorse or promote this organization, nor does it warrant the accuracy or quality of the content contained herein.

data.title: Chevron: Buffett's Largest Energy Holding Looks Inexpensive
data.description:
The Q2 earnings season is over and the common theme that I recognized was that the AI story is far from over. The allocation toward CapEx continues to expand and more companies are looking for ways to cash in on the AI segment. I don’t believe that the technology segment is the only sector that will benefit from AI and that energy will be an indirect beneficiary from the increased demand. Chevron Corporation (NYSE:CVX) is Warren Buffett’s largest energy company as it represented 6.79% of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s (BRK.A) (BRK.B) portfolio at the end of Q2 with Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) following with a 4.81% allocation. CVX has a premier asset portfolio that is vertically integrated through its upstream, midstream, and downstream segments. I believe that CVX is undervalued as it is expected to expand its profitability due to the increasing demand for traditional energy sources. CVX has a large moat around its business, as its scale allows them to have low breakeven points per barrel. I believe that the current valuation is attractive as investors could experience significant appreciation in the share price while getting paid more than 4.25% from the annualized dividend in an interest rate environment that is declining.

Following up on my previous article about Chevron

In July of 2024, I wrote an article on CVX (can be read here) where I discussed why I felt shares of CVX were headed higher. The S&P has done exceptionally well since then as it has increased 19.01% while shares of CVX have only appreciated by 3.6%. When the dividend is accounted for CVX’s total return has been 9.51%. I felt that CVX offered a strong value opportunity as it was becoming abundantly clear that we needed more oil and gas not less of it. Companies were starting to ramp up their CapEx allocations toward data centers and U.S. oil production was starting to break out above 13 million bpd again. I am following up with a new article on CVX as I believe there is a lot of forward potential for this investment case. CVX has completed its acquisition of HESS, achieved record production levels, entered the U.S. lithium sector, and is trading at a low valuation. I think that shares of CVX will become more popular as the Fed lowers rates due to their dividend yield which exceeds 4.25%. Income investors are likely to move capital from the sidelines back into the capital markets as rates decline and the fact that CVX has increased its dividend for 37 years will provide

Chevron looks undervalued at the current valuation and I believe that the energy landscape is supporting a strong outlook

When I compare CVX to the other supermajors and some other dividend staples, including The Coca-Cola Company (KO), The Procter & Gamble Company (PG), and Walmart Inc. (WMT), I believe that CVX looks inexpensive. CVX is expected to generate $7.87 of EPS in 2025 putting its valuation at 20.18 times 2025 earnings. Over the next 2 years CVX is expected to grow its EPS to $11.68 which is an increase of 48.41%. CVX is trading at 13.6 times 2027 earnings which is a bit higher than Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) but it has significantly more growth on the horizon. I think that investors can get a lot of growth at a low valuation with shares of CVX and they are also getting rewarded through the robust dividend program. CVX is paying an annualized dividend of $6.84 which is a 4.27% yield based on the current share price. CVX has increased the dividend for the past 37 years, and the dividend growth rate is 5.96% over the past 5 years. Investors can lock in a dividend yield that is exceeding the 10-year with annualized dividend increases. I think there is a lot of value to be unlocked as shares of CVX could generate a lot of capital appreciation while providing significant amounts of dividend income.

When I look at the energy landscape it looks to support the analyst projections about CVX’s forward EPS. The U.S. is producing the most oil in its history at 13.6 million bpd and CVX is playing a significant role in this. According to the EIA the global production and consumption levels will continue to increase throughout the remainder of 2025 and through 2026. This is very bullish for CVX as there will be significant demand for barrels being produced from the new growth projects it brings online. These projections could also be underestimated depending on how much the allocation towards CapEx changes from the hyperscalers over the next several years.

When I look at the amount of capital being allocated toward CapEx from Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) it's not slowing down. The bullish aspect for CVX and the energy industry is that more energy is required during construction and post-construction as it takes a large amount of electricity to run a data center. The combination of their CapEx in Q2 2025 amounted to $88.25 billion. This was an increase of 66.97% ($35.39 billion) YoY. The growth rate that these companies are spending to build out data centers is expanding considering that in Q2 2024 the combination of these 4 companies increased their CapEx spend QoQ by 19.33% ($8.56 billion) to $52.85 billion. I think there are many energy companies that will benefit from the AI revolution and CVX is in a prime position to benefit from the data center expansion that is currently occurring.

Chevron delivered a strong Q2 and I believe it's positioned for growth

CVX delivered a record-breaking quarter for production which was supported by averaging more than 1 million bpd of oil equivalent in the Permian Basin. This showcased CVX’s ability to execute a strategic vision as they introduced the timeline to reaching 1 million BPD 5 years ago. CVX has been drilling in the Permian for over a century as they have more than 2 million net acres and significant mineral interests that span roughly 75% of its total acreage in the basin. CVX has almost doubled their oil production over the past 5 years while improving well designs, reducing cycle times, and developing new technology that has led to a 30% reduction in development and production unit costs. CVX’s global portfolio has demonstrated exceptional performance as their first growth project at the Tengiz (TCO) asset in Kazakhstan is now producing at full rates, and the facility is operating 18% above its original expected capacity. In the Gulf of America, production is ramping up from their recent major project start-ups, while improved recovery techniques on base assets have enhanced estimated ultimate recovery by 9%. CVX’s Australian LNG facilities are operating 7% above their original capacity as well. CVX’s downstream business also recognized its highest U.S. refinery crude throughput in over 20 years. CVX has made these achievements through its optimization efforts and top-quartile turnaround performance.

I believe that CVX has positioned itself for growth as it has undertaken significant strategic initiatives that are designed to enhance efficiency, reduce costs, and position the company for new avenues of growth. CVX has restructured its organization and reduced the number of upstream reporting units by approximately 70% by grouping together similar asset classes like the shale and tight businesses in the Permian, DJ, Bakken, and Argentina. This is intended to scale best practices faster, standardize solutions, and streamline support functions. CVX is expanding centralized engineering hubs and global business services to drive further standardization and value. These changes are expected to achieve $2 billion to $3 billion in structural cost reductions by the end of 2026. In Q2 CVX took its first step into the U.S. lithium sector as they acquired approximately 125,000 net acres of lithium-rich land in Texas and Arkansas. CVX is planning to establish a scalable domestic business in direct lithium extraction to further diversify its growth opportunities. CVX was able to increase its frontier acreage portfolio by over 20% in the last two years and secured new offshore exploration blocks in Brazil and Egypt and has wells planned for Suriname, Namibia, and Egypt by the end of 2025. CVX has also entered into long-term contracts in its liquefied natural gas ((LNG) segment, which will increase its total U.S. Gulf Coast offtake capacity to 7 million tonnes per year.

I believe that CVX’s forward growth will be expedited by the Hess acquisition as it will create a premier international energy company by adding world-class assets. Hess’s portfolio allowed CVX to expand its shale portfolio and the combined upstream portfolio is now projected to lead the industry in total cash generation through the remainder of the decade. This deal is expected to be cash flow accretive by Q4 2025 and CVX is accelerating the integration process and expects to realize the full $1 billion in annual run-rate synergies by the end of 2025 which is 6 months ahead of the original guidance. CVX’s total shale and tight oil production is expected to increase to 1.6 million BOE/D which represents 40% of their total output. CVX’s focus for its shale assets is shifting from growth to generating substantial FCF by holding production levels, which will support making investments throughout its entire portfolio. This will help CVX maintain its balance sheet and fund their capital allocation program back to shareholders. CVX is now projecting that through the integration of HESS they will be able to increase their FCF by $12.5 billion in 2026. I am exceptionally bullish on CVX because the increased FCF allows them to expand their growth initiatives when opportunities present themselves.

Conclusion

I think that CVX is an undervalued gem that isn’t getting enough attention because of the segment it operates in. If CVX were a technology company it would have a much larger multiple considering the analyst community is projecting that they will grow EPS by almost 50% over the next 2 years. CVX is positioned to thrive over the next several years as more data centers come online and the HESS acquisition expands their footprint. The Fed just started to cut rates again which could stimulate the economy and also drive energy utilization higher causing CVX’s earnings to expand. CME Group is expecting that we could see rates decline by another 125 bps by the end of next year which would make the dividend yield from CVX of 4.27% much more attractive because risk-free assets will continuously generate less income while CVX raises the dividend. Ultimately, I think CVX is undervalued and there is tremendous value for investors to unlock as shares could go higher through next year.
